title: Interview with Dude Living in Van in Tucson 11-2-2010

transcript.text: So here I am hanging out at a nice trailhead and I met David who lives out of his van like me.
So let's check out the setup. What kind of car is this?
He used to have a TV, VCR, Super Nintendo all up there.
All that stuff. I had to use the TV wire to bypass the starter and run this button to it.
Like, turn the key, press the button.
Uh-huh. Do you see that thing on the back of my van? That's that solar panel.
Yeah, and it charges my inverter, my RV battery, which is connected to my inverter.
So I have like six outlets in my van where I can plug in all my computer and my MP3 players.
So I can like be in the middle of nowhere for like a month and I can have a computer and, you know, MP3 the whole time.
Yeah, yeah. That's pretty cool, man.
Yeah, like, and I only have one panel.
Like, if you were to cover your whole roof with panels, you would have enough energy to be watching TV, playing Nintendo all the time.
It needs new tubes and stuff.
I don't even know if it's, I don't know shit about bikes, but we'll just go.
If you need tubes, I can hook you up with a couple.
I've got all the tools if you want to fix it right now.
So, yeah, you can save a lot of money having a bicycle if you live out of your van because you just ride your bike around instead.
Yeah, I park you at one place in town and the rest of the time I just roll around.
I had another bike, but it kind of ate the dust.
So, how long have you lived out of your van for?
Um, I don't know, five years, five or six years.
Out of that five years, how many months did you actually like live in a house, like couch surfing or whatever?
Um, I would say on average per year, three to four months.
I was with this woman for a little while and we lived together for about five months.
So, as long as I lived anywhere, then we split up.
Yeah, well, the temperature's perfect out here.
So, do you cook your own food and stuff?
Yeah, I cook my own food and, um, hunt when I can.
If I'm not in the desert, then I fish and just try to, like, live off the land as much as I can.
When I'm in Washington, I just forage all the time.
Like, there's all kinds of stuff to live on up there.
All the wild plants and, yeah.
So, between, like, fishing and trapping and foraging.
You know, I heard there's a way you can eat groundhog or, like, rodents if you want.
What you do is you put a cooking stove up and you prop it up with a stick.
And then you, you know, you pull the stick when he runs in.
And then you just pour, like, cooking gas all around it and light it.
And it sucks all the oxygen out.
So, when you open up the thing, he's already dead.
You don't have to, like, fight him.
I had a friend who lived out in Alaska for three weeks and all he ate was, like, groundhogs.
When I'm watching, I eat a lot of rabbits.
Yeah, you can only live off of rabbits, though, if you eat the bones, too, because the meat alone
Isn't, like, sustaining enough to keep you going.
But with the bones, too, and, like, the heart, you're pretty good.
Can you feed yourself pretty easily fishing, too?
Okay, I'm going to try that, for sure.
Yeah, here's really not a lot of options.
You can go up on Mount Lemmon.
There's a lake up there that keeps stalked.
But most people who live up here, though, it's like, oh, Mount Lemmon is heaven.
I bet you it's cool all year round up there.
Middle of the summer, it's like 114 down here.
It's about 80, 90 up there, which, when you go from here to up there, you notice a huge
I'm from Colorado, and the road up there is just as good as, you know, the best stuff
So, uh, so that's cool that you can legally sleep inside Tucson.
A lot of people do it, especially this time of year, as I was saying.
And there's a, uh, Walmart in the south part of Tucson that I think legally lets you park
Yeah, there's another one in the north Tucson, I think, that lets you, too.
The one in north, is that the one that's near the Starbucks and the Taco Bell?
I slept in that the other day, and it bugged me.
Usually, I'll do, like, hotel parking lots, usually, because you kind of blend in there,
No one really, like, asks you if you're going through something, your van is like, oh, I'm just
Looking for something, you know, it'll bug you.
Yeah, and, uh, you can pull curtains on this and stuff.
Yeah, yeah, I gotta fix this one, but, you know, it's all good.
I was parked in the Walgreens over there on, like, Speedway and Kolb, and, like, these
Cops cuffed me and put me in their car because this lady thought I stole her, uh, her purse.
Because I guess these black dudes, like, solar person was buying stuff there, and so she,
Anyways, I told them I was in my van, they seemed to think it was a cool idea, and they
They were just telling me, they didn't even care if I slept in Walgreens as long as I got
So I went in there, and I asked the people for permission, and he was like, yeah, sure,
But then that night, the security guard woke me up, and he's like, but the sign says, and
Right behind me, it said, no RV parking.
But, uh, I slept there anyway because my car wouldn't start, so.
It was in the shop the last three days.
I rented this, like, little, like, 2010 Mazda 3, driving it all over the mountains.
Yeah, I was pulled over once by these cops in Durango, and they said all I had to do
Was sleep, like, right outside the same limits, and I was okay.
But I got a $40 fine for sleeping in the campus of my university one.
Yeah, I've never had, the only time I've had problems in here was in Washington.
I was about to start a job with a park service, and I just, like, I'm just going to, like,
Park, I was meeting at their headquarters at 5 a.m., so I was like, oh, I'm just going
They came, like, ten of them all over the line.
So I'm surrounding the van, tapping on the window, hey, where are you?
I'm like, oh, I'm sorry, I work for you guys.
Actually, I'm supposed to start tomorrow, and I just, you know, live in my van, so.
They were just, like, park here, wake up, go in.
They were like, oh, okay, well, yeah, just, you know.
That's a good thing to say.
God, you guys got a lot of guys out here for this.
Yeah, I was sleeping in the Riddle car the other night, and this cop, I woke up, and this cop
Was right next to me in his car, and I went to sleep, and he never bothered me,
So I guess he was just checking the tags or something.
But that was, like, right in town.
But it was, it had a lot of space to pull out.
I mean, Tucson is perfect, man.
A lot of the cops are pretty cool here.
I've only had, like, one bad exchange.
Yeah, the four guys I talked to were really nice.
It's just, like, I don't know.
A lot of them think that, like, the laws here are so stupid.
Like, with the immigration law, the SB 1070, they're just, like, oh, they think it's so
The immigration laws, like, where it's basically, like, racial profiling.
So, if, like, our violinist in the band is Mexican, and he's gotten hassled so many times,
Because they can use it just based on the color of your skin to, like, ask you for identification
Or whatever, and they, like, now have the right, or it's, the federal law is probably
Going to push it over, but they, um, they, if they pull you over, then apparently they're
Allowed to ask you about other crimes aside from the grounds for pulling you over, which
Is, like, total, like, breach of...
Well, the cops, the cops who told me, they asked me if I was, had drugs, and, uh, they,
Yeah, but I think that's the only thing they asked me.
No, yeah, he asked me if I ever, ever been arrested.
The other guy told me it was illegal for me to beg for money.
You have to leave the city limits for that.
They say it's illegal, but down on Fourth Avenue, man, it's, like, cops walk by all the
I'll sit up there with my hat and a bunch of money, and the cops walk by, and they just, like,
Look down and walk past, and there's, like, ten feet down from me, there's, like, two
Panhandlers, just, like, a pair of them.
I'll always hang out, ask them for my, hey, spare any change?
Yeah, the lady who asked me for money in that parking lot was nice.
Well, I can, uh, help you get those, uh, tubes if you want.
